core_meaning: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える means something is visible or can be seen — it describes the passive, spontaneous experience of vision rather than the active act of looking. It also carries a second meaning of 'to appear' or 'to seem,' used when something gives a certain impression.

sentences.jp: <ruby>窓<rt>まど</rt></ruby>から<ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えるよ！
sentences.translation: You can see Mt. Fuji from the window!
sentences.notes: Core use: something comes into view naturally — the mountain is simply there to be seen, no effort implied.

sentences.jp: <ruby>霧<rt>きり</rt></ruby>が<ruby>濃<rt>こ</rt></ruby>くて、ほとんど<ruby>何<rt>なに</rt></ruby>も<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えなかった。
sentences.translation: The fog was so thick that I could barely see anything.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える used in the negative past to describe an inability to perceive — a very natural collocation.

sentences.jp: あの<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>、<ruby>実際<rt>じっさい</rt></ruby>より<ruby>若<rt>わか</rt></ruby>く<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えるね。
sentences.translation: That person looks younger than they actually are, don't they?
sentences.notes: The 'appear / seem' sense — describing the impression someone or something gives based on appearance.

sentences.jp: このグラフを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>ると、<ruby>景気<rt>けいき</rt></ruby>の<ruby>回復<rt>かいふく</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えてきます。
sentences.translation: Looking at this graph, the economic recovery is beginning to become visible.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えてくる is a useful pattern meaning a situation or trend gradually becomes apparent or discernible.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>努力<rt>どりょく</rt></ruby>は<ruby>端<rt>はた</rt></ruby>から<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>ても<ruby>明<rt>あき</rt></ruby>らかに<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えていた。
sentences.translation: Her effort was clearly apparent even to those watching from the outside.
sentences.notes: Shows <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える in a more abstract, figurative sense — something becoming evident or manifest to observers.

dont_use: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える is for spontaneous or passive perception — don't use it when you mean the deliberate act of looking at something; use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る for that. Also avoid using <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える for non-visual impressions like sound or smell — for those, use <ruby>聞<rt>き</rt></ruby>こえる or <ruby>感<rt>かん</rt></ruby>じる. Don't confuse the 'seem' sense with <ruby>思<rt>おも</rt></ruby>う — <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える implies the impression is based on outward appearance, not an internal opinion.

confused_with.contrast: A common learner mistake is saying 「<ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えます」when they mean 'I can see the mountain' — this is unnatural because <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える is intransitive and takes が, not を. The correct form is 「<ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えます」. Use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る when you actively direct your gaze (「テレビを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る」), and <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える when something is simply visible or gives an impression.

frequency: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える is very common in everyday speech across all registers — both the 'be visible' sense and the 'appear/seem' sense appear constantly in conversation, writing, and media. The <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えてくる pattern is especially frequent in news and analytical writing.
anki_hint: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える takes が (not を) because it's spontaneous and intransitive — something naturally enters your field of vision or gives an impression, you don't aim your eyes at it.
